metadata.summary: Flag service landscape (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Unleash, OpenFeature, Piranha), pytest fixtures for both branches, stale-flag CI audit workflow, and agentic workflow for flag generation and cleanup.

content.title: Feature Flag Services, Testing, and Agentic Workflow
content.summary: Choosing a flag service, writing pytest fixtures that test both ON and OFF code paths, running a weekly stale-flag CI audit, and wiring a flag agent that generates definitions, wraps new code paths, and creates cleanup tasks are the four operational concerns that complete a production-grade flag system.

why.p: Testing only the enabled path is the single most common feature-flag bug. An untested disabled path is a latent production incident waiting for the flag to be turned off. Pytest fixtures that explicitly exercise both branches make this failure mode a lint-level catch rather than a production incident.

Without automated stale-flag detection, cleanup never happens. A weekly CI job that lists flags past their expiry and opens a GitHub issue converts a social obligation ("someone should clean this up") into a trackable work item with an owner.

list.item: Choosing between flag service vendors for a new project or migration.
Writing tests that must cover both the enabled and disabled code path for a flagged feature.
Setting up a weekly CI audit to surface flags past their expected cleanup date.
Configuring an AI agent to generate flag definitions, wrap new code, write dual-branch tests, and register cleanup tasks automatically.
Single-flag prototypes — the overhead of a full service integration outweighs the value.
Projects that have no CI pipeline — the stale-flag audit workflow assumes a scheduled GitHub Actions runner.

section.name: service-landscape
section.p: Flag services differ on self-hostability, agent-friendliness (REST API quality), experimentation support, and solo-developer pricing. The OpenFeature CNCF spec decouples call sites from the vendor so the provider can be swapped without rewriting the application.

section.name: service-table
section.p: Service comparison for solo and small-team use:

list.item: LaunchDarkly — SaaS only; industry default; best REST API + CLI; expensive at scale. Agent-friendly.
Flagsmith — SaaS + OSS self-hostable; good REST; comparable features at lower cost. Agent-friendly.
Unleash — SaaS + OSS; strong gradual rollout strategies; active community. Agent-friendly REST.
GrowthBook — SaaS + OSS; bundles flags + A/B experimentation + warehouse integration. Excellent for experiment-heavy teams.
ConfigCat — SaaS; solo-friendly free tier; simplest model; no self-host.
PostHog Feature Flags — SaaS + OSS; bundled with product analytics; good if you already use PostHog.
Statsig — SaaS; strongest on experimentation and CUPED variance reduction.
OpenFeature (CNCF spec) — vendor-neutral SDK; use via a provider; avoids lock-in. Strongly recommended for new projects.
Cloudflare Workers KV — DIY edge flags via KV API; no targeting UI; use only if already on Workers.

rule.statement: New projects MUST use the OpenFeature SDK so the provider can be swapped without rewriting call sites. The provider (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, custom) is wired once at startup.
rule.rationale: Vendor-specific SDKs scatter proprietary calls across the codebase. Migrating later requires touching every flag evaluation point. OpenFeature wraps the provider behind a stable interface.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: CLI tools for flag management from the command line or CI scripts:

list.item: unleash-cli — Manage Unleash flags from CLI: npm i -g unleash-cli
ld-cli — LaunchDarkly CLI: brew install launchdarkly/tap/ldcli
flagsmith-cli — Flagsmith CLI: npm i -g flagsmith-cli
growthbook (CLI) — OSS A/B + flags: npm i -g growthbook
openfeature SDK — Vendor-neutral flag API: pip install openfeature-sdk
piranha (Uber) — Auto-cleanup of stale flags: https://github.com/uber/piranha

section.name: testing-both-branches
section.p: Every flagged feature MUST have tests for both the enabled and disabled code path. Pytest fixtures that toggle overrides on the global FeatureFlagManager instance make this mechanical.

example.title: Pytest fixtures for flag-enabled and flag-disabled paths
example.note: The parametrized form is the most concise way to guarantee both paths are exercised. Use it when the behavior differs only in which branch runs, not in the assertions.

rule.statement: Every new flag MUST have a test with the flag enabled AND a test with the flag disabled before the PR is merged. Code coverage tools MUST treat an untested branch as a failure.
rule.rationale: Agents frequently write only the enabled-path test because that is the "happy path." The disabled path is the fallback that runs for 99% of users during initial rollout — it must work.

section.name: stale-flag-audit
section.p: A weekly GitHub Actions workflow runs a stale-flag finder script and opens an issue listing flags past their expected cleanup date. This converts cleanup from a social obligation into a tracked work item.

example.title: Stale flag finder script and CI workflow
example.code:
# scripts/find_stale_flags.py — list flags older than N days import json import subprocess import sys from datetime import datetime, timedelta
DAYS = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 90 cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=DAYS)
flags = json.loads(subprocess.check_output( ["ld-cli", "flags", "list", "--output", "json"] ))
for f in flags: last = datetime.fromisoformat(f["lastModified"].replace("Z", "+00:00")) if last.replace(tzinfo=None) < cutoff: usage = subprocess.run( ["git", "grep", "-l", f["key"]], capture_output=True, text=True ).stdout.strip().splitlines() print(f"STALE: {f['key']:40s} last={last:%Y-%m-%d} files={len(usage)}")

example.title: Weekly CI stale-flag audit workflow
example.code: # .github/workflows/flag-audit.yml — weekly stale-flag check on: schedule: [{ cron: '0 9 * * 1' }] jobs: audit: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - run: python scripts/find_stale_flags.py 90 > stale.txt - run: gh issue create --title "Stale flags audit $(date -I)" --body-file stale.txt env: { GH_TOKEN: '${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}' }
example.note: The issue is created only when stale.txt is non-empty — add a check if stale.txt is empty, skip gh issue create to avoid noise.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A flag agent automates the full flag lifecycle: generates definitions, wraps new code paths, writes dual-branch tests, and registers cleanup tasks in the SDD backlog.
section.prompt: New flag: <name>. Type: release|experiment|ops|permission|kill-switch. Owner: <handle>. Expiry: <YYYY-MM-DD>. Default in code: false (release/experiment) or true (kill-switch). Generate: 1. Flag definition in flags.py / flags.ts. 2. Wrap the new branch with is_enabled(). 3. Two test files: tests/<feature>_on_test.py, tests/<feature>_off_test.py. 4. SDD cleanup task in todo/ with expiry date in title.

prompt.purpose: audit and propose actions for stale flags

section.prompt: Audit flags: list all flags with expiry < today() OR last_changed > 90 days ago. For each: propose REMOVE if rollout=100%, ROLLBACK if errors>baseline, EXTEND with justification.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — gates on "both flag branches covered by tests" and "flag has expiry".
faion-improver (skill) — periodic audit pass to surface stale flags and propose removals.

antipattern.description: Agent forgets the OFF-branch test entirely, writing only the happy-path test.
antipattern.reason: Force the gate: faion-sdd-executor-agent MUST require both branches tested before marking the task done.

antipattern.description: Removal is harder than addition: agent deletes the flag check but leaves the OFF branch orphaned in the code.
antipattern.reason: Use piranha or a two-step review: first confirm the flag is at 100%, then delete both the check and the old branch in the same commit.

antipattern.description: During incidents, agent flips the wrong flag because flag keys are ambiguous or similarly named.
antipattern.reason: Require explicit flag key + dry-run output before any production flag flip. Human-in-loop for kill-switch operations.

antipattern.description: Cross-service flag drift: same flag name, different semantics in service A vs service B.
antipattern.reason: User sees broken state when service A treats the flag as enabled and service B treats it as disabled. Lock the registry and validate flag name uniqueness across services in CI.
